text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the mesentery from a caudal to cranial direction, carefully separating the mesenteric tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the mesenteric window, creating an access point through the mesentery during the laparoscopic low anterior resection.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein, carefully dissecting it from surrounding tissues using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon ligates the isolated inferior mesenteric vein with two hemoclips and then cuts the vein, completing the ligation process.
The surgeon cuts the inferior mesenteric artery using hemoclips, similar to the previous ligation technique.
The ureter is visible below the surgical field during the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies a vascular plane on the posterior aspect of the rectum, revealing distinct tissue layers and vascular structures.
The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side of the rectum, maintaining the separation of tissue planes.
The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt, creating a visible separation along the lateral peritoneal reflection.
The surgeon exposes the spine during the dissection, revealing its anatomical position relative to the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the left colon, ensuring it is fully freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon encounters adhesions in the surgical field, likely resulting from previous procedures, which are visible as fibrous tissue bands connecting anatomical structures.
The surgeon visually inspects a suspicious area for endometriosis, examining the tissue closely to confirm its presence.
The surgeon performs a colonoscopy, inserting the colonoscope into the patient's colon to visually inspect the internal lining.
The surgeon confirms the correct anatomical location and identifies clean surgical margins for resection.
The surgeon prepares the rectum for anastomosis, ensuring the area is ready for the surgical connection of the bowel segments.
The surgeon transects the distal section of the rectum using GA steppers, completing the separation of the tissue.
The surgeon carefully preserves the left ureter, ensuring it remains intact and undamaged during the procedure.
The surgeon observes signs of fibrosis on the rectum, which is prepared for anastomosis.
The surgeon checks for hemostasis, ensuring there is no active bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the prepared rectum to the colon.
